3	description: Whole-blueprint audit. Per-chapter checklist of completeness + correctness plus summaries of which parts are incomplete, which proofs lack detail, whether Lean targets are well-formulated, and whether multi-route strategies have coverage for every route. Also audits STRATEGY.md phases for blueprint coverage gaps and proposes concrete chapter outlines for phases not yet started, so the plan agent can immediately dispatch a blueprint-writer rather than defer.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"

8	mandatory: [plan, dag]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - Dispatch me BEFORE writing any prover objectives or touching Lean files.
11	    The plan agent's job is to make sure the blueprint is complete and
12	    detailed enough first; only then should provers be assigned. A weak
13	    blueprint produces low-quality prover work that the next iter then
14	    has to throw away.
15	  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. The audit warning fires
16	    if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
17	
18	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
19	      - no chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/` was edited since my
20	        prior dispatch (check via `git diff --stat HEAD~N
21	        blueprint/src/chapters/` where N spans iters back to my last
22	        run);
23	      - my prior verdict cleared the HARD GATE for all chapters
24	        currently under active prover work;
25	      - no must-fix-this-iter finding from my prior dispatch remains
26	        live (every flagged chapter has either been writer-patched or
27	        dropped from objectives).
28	
29	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
30	    with the one-line rationale. Do NOT skip me when the prior verdict
31	    flagged any chapter `partial | false` and that chapter still feeds
32	    a live prover lane — the HARD GATE depends on a current audit.
33	  - I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting
34	    directive — even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-
35	    chapter view is the point of running me.
36	  - Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters
37	    need a follow-up writer dispatch this iter (consult your catalog
38	    for the blueprint-writing subagent). You do not need to re-read
39	    the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.
40	  - **Act on my unstarted-phase proposals.** My report's
41	    `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals` section names every
42	    strategy phase with no blueprint coverage and provides a concrete
43	    chapter outline for each. These are not informational — treat
44	    each proposal as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Dispatch a
45	    blueprint-writing subagent for each proposed chapter this iter
46	    (or record an explicit deferral rationale in the iter sidecar).
47	    Do NOT let unstarted phases accumulate across iters: a phase
48	    with no blueprint is a phase that cannot be parallelised, cannot
49	    be reviewed, and cannot be handed to a prover. Writing the
50	    blueprint early is the cheapest action in the loop.
51	
52	  ### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch
53	
54	  This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
55	  Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:
56	
57	  - For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
58	    `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
59	    the corresponding blueprint chapter C. The mapping is: if some
60	    chapter declares `% archon:covers ... F ...` near its top, THAT
61	    chapter is C (a consolidated chapter blueprints several files while
62	    its siblings are thin pointers); otherwise fall back to the 1:1
63	    `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug. Look up C in my per-chapter
64	    checklist. When a chapter declares `covers:`, its single verdict
65	    gates EVERY file it lists — so a `correct: false` on the
66	    consolidated chapter defers all of them, and one writer directive
67	    against that chapter fixes the content behind all of them.
68	  - If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
69	    finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
70	  - Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
71	    finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
72	    blueprint depends on):
73	    1. DROP F from this iter's objectives (by default — but see the
74	       same-iter fast path below).
75	    2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
76	       iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
77	       flagged.
78	    3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was at risk and what the
79	       writer was asked to fix.
80	  - **Same-iter fast path (sanctioned — removes a wasted iter).** After
81	    the writer returns AND `lake build` is green, you MAY re-dispatch me
82	    scoped to C alone — a fresh slug, a directive naming only C and the
83	    must-fix items I flagged. If that scoped re-review returns C as
84	    `complete: true` AND `correct: true` with no must-fix finding, the
85	    gate is satisfied for C: add F to THIS iter's objectives and send a
86	    prover. No need to burn an iter waiting. If the scoped re-review
87	    still fails, F stays deferred.
88	  - If you do NOT take the fast path, F simply waits: the next iter's
89	    mandatory dispatch of me re-confirms C before any prover runs on F.
90	    The fast path is purely a latency optimization — it never weakens
91	    the gate. F enters objectives ONLY on a fresh `complete + correct`
92	    verdict with no must-fix, whether that verdict arrives this iter (via
93	    the fast path) or next (via the mandatory dispatch). A green build
94	    alone is NOT sufficient; the re-review is what clears the gate.
95	
96	  ### Strategy / multi-route handling
97	
98	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
99	    or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
100	    blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
101	    in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
102	    until its blueprint coverage is in place.
103	  - If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
104	    treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
105	    — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
106	    in the blueprint.
107	
108	  ### What "deferred" means in practice
109	
110	  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
111	  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
112	  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
113	  the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
114	  move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.
115	---
116	
117	# Blueprint Reviewer
118	
119	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
120	
121	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
122	
123	## Your Job
124	
125	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
126	
127	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
128	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
129	- **Dependency-graph integrity (use `leandag`)** — the `\uses{}` edges must reflect the real mathematical dependencies. `leandag` computes the actual DAG, so use it instead of eyeballing:
130	  - **Broken edges** — `leandag build --json` reports `unknown_uses` (a `\uses{}` pointing at a label no declaration defines). Every one is a must-fix.
131	  - **Missing edges** — read each proof: if it relies on a lemma/definition that its `\uses{}` does not list, the edge is missing. These don't show up as "broken"; you find them by reading the math against the declared `\uses{}`. Missing edges make the loop dispatch provers out of order, so flag them as correctness findings.
132	  - **Isolated declarations** — `leandag show isolated` (or `leandag query --isolated --type theorem`, and the `isolated` / `isolated_blueprint` counts in `leandag stats`) lists nodes with **no `\uses{}` out and nothing using them**. An isolated *blueprint* node is almost always a *symptom of a missing edge*, not dead weight — so for each one decide and record a disposition (see below). Isolated `lean_aux` nodes are uncovered Lean helpers, a separate "needs a blueprint entry" signal, not removal candidates.
133	- **Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)** — it is good practice for a chapter that relies on a Mathlib-provided result to state it as an explicit anchor block marked `\mathlibok` (with `\lean{}` naming the real Mathlib declaration), rather than a bare `\uses{}` to nothing. Treat such anchors as *valid and done* — never flag them as ∞ holes or `remove` candidates. But **audit each `\mathlibok` claim for faithfulness**, because a wrong one makes the loop skip a real gap: does Mathlib actually contain the named `\lean{}` declaration, and does the stated form match it? Verify against Lean (`archon-lean-lsp`) when in doubt. A `\mathlibok` on a statement Mathlib does not provide — or whose form is stronger/different than Mathlib's — is a hard fail (treat like a fabricated citation). Also: where a broken `\uses{}` points at what is really a Mathlib result, the right `wire-up` is "add a `\mathlibok` anchor for it," not "invent a project lemma."
134	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
135	- **Rendering integrity (run `archon blueprint-doctor`)** — the blueprint must *render*: as the compiled leanblueprint site and in the dashboard. The doctor lints this deterministically — run `archon blueprint-doctor --json` once and triage every `malformed_refs` finding into your report:
136	  - **`undefined-macro`** — a chapter uses `\foo` that no `macros/*.tex` or chapter-local `\providecommand` defines: it renders as raw TeX everywhere. Recommend the definition (usually obvious from context: `\Z` → `\mathbb{Z}`, `\fppf` → `\mathrm{fppf}`); when the intent is genuinely unclear (you cannot tell what the writer meant), flag it as a correctness finding for the chapter's writer instead of guessing.
137	  - **`math-delim`** — interleaved `$ … \( … \) … $` delimiters that shred formulas mid-sentence; per-line locations are in the finding. Must-fix, writer-directive material.
138	  - **`literal-ref` / `bare-label`** — placeholder "REF" tokens and raw label ids in prose; each needs a `\cref{}` or the human-readable number.
139	  Group these per chapter in your findings so the dispatcher can scope one writer per damaged chapter.
140	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
141	- **Unstarted phases** — for each phase in the strategy that has no blueprint chapter at all, produce a concrete chapter outline (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" below). This is a proactive planning function, not just a gap report.
142	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
143	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
144	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
145	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
146	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
147	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
148	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
149	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
150	
151	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
152	
153	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
154	
155	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
156	
157	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
158	
159	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematically correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
160	
161	## Always read everything
162	
163	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
164	
165	## Unstarted-phase proposals
166	
167	This is a proactive function — you produce it in addition to the per-chapter audit, not instead of it.
168	
169	After completing the per-chapter checklist, cross-reference the strategy snapshot's `## Phases & estimations` table against the set of chapters you just read. (That table lists only *remaining* phases — finished ones live in `## Completed` and need no proposal.) For each remaining phase row whose content has **zero blueprint coverage** (no chapter exists, or the only existing chapter is a stub with fewer than ~3 meaningful declaration blocks), produce a **chapter outline proposal**.
170	
171	A chapter outline proposal is not a flag or a complaint — it is a concrete, actionable seed that the plan agent can hand directly to a blueprint-writing subagent. It must contain enough mathematical detail that a writer receiving it as a directive can produce a complete chapter without further research by the plan agent.
172	
173	The proposal answers:
174	
175	1. **Which Lean file(s) will this chapter cover?** Name the expected file path(s) and whether this should be a consolidated chapter (one chapter covering multiple files via `% archon:covers`) or a 1:1 chapter.
176	
177	2. **What are the declaration blocks the chapter needs?** For each: the block type (definition / lemma / theorem / proposition), a proposed `\label`, a one-sentence description of the mathematical content, the expected `\lean{...}` hint (even if speculative — tag it `[expected]`), and the likely reference source (from `references/summary.md` or a named standard reference). Do not write the full prose — that is the writer's job — but give enough that the writer knows exactly what to write.
178	
179	3. **What are the internal dependencies?** Which declarations within this chapter use others (the `\uses{...}` graph in skeletal form). Identifying this upfront prevents the writer from writing declarations in an order that creates circular `\uses` references.
180	
181	4. **What is the proof strategy for the chapter's main theorem(s)?** Two to four sentences naming the key steps, the supporting lemmas, and any Mathlib infrastructure the proofs will depend on. This is the piece most likely to require a reference; if you can identify the relevant section of a source in `references/summary.md`, name it.
182	
183	5. **What references should the blueprint-writer read first?** List the specific local files under `references/` that are most relevant, and the sections within them. If no local file covers the material, flag it as a retrieval need so the plan agent can dispatch a reference-retriever before the writer.
184	
185	6. **Are there subphase choices the outline exposes?** If the chapter's material admits multiple natural decompositions (e.g. the main theorem could be approached via method A or method B, or the definition could be bundled or unbundled), name them explicitly. The plan agent cannot make an informed subphase choice without seeing what the choices are — surfacing them here is the primary value of writing the blueprint early rather than late.
186	
187	**Format for each proposal** (one block per unstarted phase):
188	
189	```
190	### Proposed chapter: <expected filename, e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Foo_Bar.tex`>
191	
192	**Covers**: `Foo/Bar.lean` (+ any sibling files if consolidated)
193	**Strategy phase**: <phase name from STRATEGY.md>
194	**Why now**: <one sentence on why writing this chapter this iter, before any prover work, is the right move — what ambiguity it resolves, what parallelism it enables>
195	
196	**Key declarations** (in dependency order):
197	1. `\definition` `\label{def:foo}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <reference + section, or "no source identified — Archon-original">

248	## What you do
249	
250	1. **Read your directive completely.** Extract the `## Phases & estimations` table — you will need it for the unstarted-phase proposals.
251	2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
252	3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
253	   - Read the entire chapter.
254	   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
255	   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
256	   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
257	4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
258	5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
259	6. **Audit the dependency graph with `leandag`.** Run `leandag build --json` (broken/unknown `\uses{}`, `unmatched_lean`, isolated count) and `leandag show isolated` once for the whole blueprint. Then triage each isolated **blueprint** node into exactly one disposition and record it:
260	   - **wire-up** — it *should* connect to the graph but an edge is missing or mislabeled. Name the `\uses{}` that should be added (and on which side). This is the common case and a correctness finding — recommend a writer fix, do NOT recommend removal.
261	   - **remove** — it is genuinely orphaned scaffolding: nothing in the goal's proof closure needs it, it isn't the goal, and it isn't a `\mathlibok`/reference anchor. Only then is removal the right call. Name the chapter + label so the plan agent can authorize a writer to delete it.
262	   - **keep** — it is intentionally standalone (the goal theorem with nothing above it, a deliberately isolated `\mathlibok` reference, etc.). Say why and move on.
263	   You **flag and recommend**; you never edit. The plan agent turns a `wire-up`/`remove` disposition into a blueprint-writer directive.
264	7. **Run the rendering lint.** `archon blueprint-doctor --json` once for the whole blueprint; triage every `malformed_refs` finding (`undefined-macro`, `math-delim`, `literal-ref`, `bare-label`) per the "Rendering integrity" bullet above, grouped per chapter.
265	8. **Check multi-route coverage**: for each route listed in the directive's `## Routes`, identify which chapters cover it. Flag any route that has zero or insufficient blueprint coverage.
266	9. **Cross-reference phases against chapters**: for each row in the strategy's `## Phases & estimations` table, determine whether adequate blueprint coverage exists. For every phase with no or stub coverage, produce a chapter outline proposal (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" above).
267	10. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.

Read-only; run it once per review and triage its `malformed_refs` (see "Rendering integrity" above).
272	- **`leandag`** — your read-only window into the real dependency DAG. Run it freely: it never touches project source or the blueprint. (`leandag build` refreshes the derived `.leandag/` cache, which is regenerated deterministically from the current tree — that is a tool artifact, not a project edit, so it does not violate your read-only rule.)
273	  - `leandag build --json` — the structured build report: `unknown_uses` (broken `\uses{}`), `unmatched_lean` (`\lean{}` pointing nowhere), `conflicts`, and `summary.isolated`.
274	  - `leandag stats` — counts including `isolated` / `isolated_blueprint`.
275	  - `leandag show isolated` (or `leandag query --isolated [--type theorem] [--chapter <c>]`) — the isolated declarations, which you triage per node (below).

282	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).

284	**Omit-empty rule.** Required sections: `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Per-chapter` (the HARD GATE depends on it), `## Severity summary`, `Overall verdict`. **Everything else is optional and must be omitted when empty.**

289	- `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals`: omit only when every phase in the strategy has adequate blueprint coverage. When present, this section is never empty — each proposal block is a concrete outline, not a one-line flag.
290	- `## Cross-chapter notes`: omit when no cross-chapter findings exist (the most common case on a clean blueprint).
291	- `## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)`: omit entirely when none. Do NOT write a section with "None" inside; the absence of the section IS the signal.

28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).

When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.

32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \

42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.

83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. 1	\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
5	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
6	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
7	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
8	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
9	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}) (read from
10	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).
11	
12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	
14	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
15	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
16	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
17	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
18	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
19	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
20	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
21	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
22	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
23	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
24	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
25	
26	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
27	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
28	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
29	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
30	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
31	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
32	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
33	\[
34	  \begin{array}{ccc}
35	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
36	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
37	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
38	  \end{array}
39	\]
40	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
41	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
42	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
43	
44	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
45	
46	\begin{definition}
47	
48	\leanok
49	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
50	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
51	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
52	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor, def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
53	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
54	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
55	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
56	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
57	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
58	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
59	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
60	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
61	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
62	  % the unit ideal, see
63	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
64	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
65	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
66	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
67	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
68	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
69	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
70	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
71	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
72	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
73	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
74	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
75	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
76	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
77	  \[
78	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
79	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
80	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
81	  \]
82	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
83	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
84	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
85	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
86	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
87	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
88	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
89	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
90	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
91	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
92	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
93	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
94	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
95	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
96	\end{definition}
97	
98	\begin{definition}
99	
100	\leanok
101	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
102	  \label{def:cech_complex}
103	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
104	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
105	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
106	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
107	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
108	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
109	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
110	  % is identified with the complex
111	  % $$
112	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
113	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
114	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
115	  % \ldots
116	  % $$
117	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
118	  % \begin{equation}
119	  % \label{equation-extended}
120	  % 0 \to
121	  % M \to
122	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
123	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
124	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
125	  % \ldots
126	  % \end{equation}
127	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
128	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
129	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
130	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
131	  vanishing).}
132	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
133	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
134	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
135	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
136	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
137	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
138	  in degree \(p\) it is
139	  \[
140	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
141	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
142	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
143	  \]
144	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
145	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
146	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
147	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
148	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
149	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
150	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
151	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
152	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
153	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
154	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
155	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
156	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
157	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
158	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
159	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
160	\end{definition}

162	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
163	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
164	
165	It is convenient to factor the construction of
166	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
167	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
168	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
169	
170	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
171	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
172	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
173	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
174	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
175	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
176	\[
177	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
178	    \;=\;
179	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
180	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
181	\]
182	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
183	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
184	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
185	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
186	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
187	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
188	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
189	
190	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
191	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
192	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
193	\[
194	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
195	  \qquad
196	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
197	\]
198	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
199	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
200	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
201	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
202	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
203	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
204	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
205	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
206	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
207	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
208	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
209	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
210	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
211	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
212	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
213	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
214	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
215	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
216	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
217	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
218	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
219	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
220	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
221	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
222	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
223	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
224	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
225	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
226	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
227	
228	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
229	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
230	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
231	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
232	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
233	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
234	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
235	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
236	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
237	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
238	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
239	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
240	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
241	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
242	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
243	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
244	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
245	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
246	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
247	
248	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
249	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
250	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
251	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
252	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
253	
254	\begin{definition}\leanok
255	[Arrow of an affine cover]
256	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
257	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
258	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
259	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
260	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
261	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
262	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
263	  apply.
264	\end{definition}

266	\begin{definition}\leanok
267	[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
268	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
269	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve}
270	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
271	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
272	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
273	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
274	  \[
275	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
276	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
277	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
278	  \]
279	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
280	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
281	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
282	\end{definition}

284	\begin{definition}\leanok
285	[Push--pull object map]
286	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
287	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
288	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
289	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
290	  \[
291	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
292	  \]
293	  the pushforward along \(p\) of the pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\).
294	\end{definition}

296	\begin{definition}\leanok
297	[Push--pull comparison map]
298	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
299	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap}
300	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
301	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
302	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
303	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
304	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
305	  \[
306	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
307	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
308	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
309	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
310	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
311	      \;=\;
312	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
313	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
314	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
315	  \]
316	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
317	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
318	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
319	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
320	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
321	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
322	\end{definition}

324	\begin{lemma}
325	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
326	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
327	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
328	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
329	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
330	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
331	\end{lemma}
332	
333	\begin{proof}
334	  A direct calculation with comparison isomorphisms, with no sectionwise content.
335	  Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward comparison as the
336	  conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison; the identity leg then collapses
337	  by the identity normalisation of the pseudofunctor together with right unitality,
338	  and the two over-triangle transport coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
339	\end{proof}

341	\begin{lemma}
342	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
343	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
344	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp}
345	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
346	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
347	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
348	  \[
349	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
350	  \]
351	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
352	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
353	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
354	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
355	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
356	\end{lemma}
357	
358	\begin{proof}
359	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
360	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
361	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
362	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of
363	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and the naturality of the adjunction unit. Second,
364	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
365	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
366	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
367	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
368	\end{proof}

370	\begin{lemma}
371	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
372	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
373	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
374	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
375	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
376	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
377	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
378	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
379	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
380	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
381	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
382	  \[
383	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
384	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
385	    \;=\;
386	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
387	  \]
388	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
389	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
390	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
391	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
392	\end{lemma}
393

395	  A direct mate-calculus computation: unfold both sides using the definition of
396	  the adjunction unit for the composite and the pseudofunctor structure maps, then
397	  verify the equality by the mate identity for the pushforward/pullback comparison.
398	\end{proof}

401	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]
402	  \label{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
403	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel}
404	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
405	  Let \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\), \(p_1 : Y_1 \to X\), \(p_2 : Y_2 \to X\) satisfy the

407	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the pullback-comparison leg of the push--pull map,
408	  conjugated by the two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle,
409	  collapses to the transport-light form
410	  \[
411	    e_1 \;\circ\; p_{2*}\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
412	      \;\circ\; e_2
413	    \;=\;
414	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
415	      \;\circ\; e_3,
416	  \]

425	\begin{proof}

427	  transport coercions by identities, then verify the cancellation by direct
428	  calculation with the pushforward comparison.

911	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules and apply \(j_*\) degreewise. Since each \(\mathcal{I}^n\)
912	  is \(j_*\)-acyclic by part (1) (\(R^q j_*\mathcal{I}^n = 0\) for \(q \geq 1\)), the
913	  complex \(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(j_*\mathcal{H}\).
914	
915	  Each \(j_*\mathcal{I}^n\) is \(f_*\)-acyclic. By the presheaf description
916	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}), \(R^k f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^n)\) is the
917	  sheafification of
918	  \[
919	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^k\bigl(f^{-1}(V), (j_*\mathcal{I}^n)|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr)
920	      \;=\; H^k\bigl(U \cap f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
921	  \]
922	  the equality because \((j_*\mathcal{I}^n)|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is the pushforward of
923	  \(\mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}\) along the open immersion
924	  \(U \cap f^{-1}(V) \hookrightarrow f^{-1}(V)\), which leaves cohomology unchanged. For
925	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec}(A) \subseteq S\) affine, \(f^{-1}(V)\) is quasi-compact and,
926	  \(X\) being separated and \(j\) affine, the affine open \(U\) meets it in the affine
927	  open \(U \cap f^{-1}(V)\); hence by Serre vanishing
928	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} the cohomology
929	  \(H^k(U \cap f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\).
930	  Since the affine opens form a basis of \(S\), this forces
931	  \(R^k f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^n) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\).
932	
933	  Thus \(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is an \(f_*\)-acyclic resolution of
934	  \(j_*\mathcal{H}\), and the acyclic-resolution comparison
935	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} applied with \(G = f_*\) gives,
936	  for every \(k\),
937	  \[
938	    R^k f_*\bigl(j_*\mathcal{H}\bigr)
939	      \;\cong\; H^k\bigl(f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)\bigr)
940	      \;=\; H^k\bigl((f \circ j)_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\bigr)
941	      \;=\; R^k g_*\mathcal{H},
942	  \]
943	  the middle equality because \(f_* \circ j_* = (f \circ j)_* = g_*\), and the last
944	  because \(\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is an injective resolution of \(\mathcal{H}\)
945	  computing \(R^k g_*\mathcal{H}\).
946	\end{proof}

974	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "According to
975	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
976	% the sheaf

978	% $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.

987	  A product of acyclic objects is acyclic, so it suffices to treat a single factor
988	  \((j_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\), where \(s = (i_0, \ldots, i_p)\), \(U_s =
989	  U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine, and \(j_s : U_s \hookrightarrow X\) is the open
990	  immersion. Acyclicity for \(f_*\) is a local question on \(S\): the derived
991	  pushforward \(R^k f_*\, \mathcal{G}\) is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
992	  \(V \mapsto H^k(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\), so it vanishes for
993	  \(k \geq 1\) iff that cohomology vanishes for all affine \(V \subseteq S\).
994	  Restricting to such a \(V = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the base change \(f_V :
995	  f^{-1}(V) \to V\) is again separated and quasi-compact over the separated base
996	  \(V\), hence \(f^{-1}(V)\) is separated and the affine \(U_s\) meets it in the
997	  affine open \(U_s \cap f^{-1}(V)\).

1000	  \(j_s\) is the inclusion of an affine open into the separated scheme \(X\), hence
1001	  an affine morphism, so the higher direct images \(R^q (j_s)_*\) vanish for
1002	  \(q \geq 1\). By Lemma~\ref{lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp} part (2),

1006	  \(U_s \cap f^{-1}(V) \to V\), which is an affine morphism; by the relative affine
1007	  vanishing \(R^k (g_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s}) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\). That relative
1008	  vanishing is itself a consequence of Serre vanishing

1010	  sheafification of \(V \mapsto H^k(g_s^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G})\), and for affine
1011	  \(V\) the preimage \(g_s^{-1}(V)\) is affine, on which higher cohomology of a
1012	  quasi-coherent sheaf vanishes. Combining, \(R^k f_*(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0\) for all
1013	  \(k \geq 1\).

1051	  \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, definition of $R^i f_*$ as the
5	% $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex
6	% and references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter records the derived-functor higher direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\)
11	for \(i \geq 1\). It provides the single object that the {\v C}ech computation of
12	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} compares against: the comparison
13	theorem \(\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}\) identifies the cohomology of the
14	relative {\v C}ech complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) wherever the derived functor
15	is defined.
16	
17	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category).
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
19	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	
21	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
22	
23	\begin{definition}\leanok
24	[Higher direct image]
25	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
26	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
27	  % NOTE (iter-233): the Lean `higherDirectImage` carries an explicit instance
28	  % hypothesis `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`, because Mathlib has
29	  % `Abelian X.Modules` but NOT `EnoughInjectives` / `IsGrothendieckAbelian` for
30	  % `SheafOfModules` over a sheaf of rings (the Grothendieck-abelian instance is
31	  % only for `Sheaf J A` with a fixed value category). The definition is therefore
32	  % conditional; the hypothesis can be dropped once a lane proves that instance.
33	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
34	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
35	  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf
36	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
37	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
38	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to
39	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
40	  % the sheaf
41	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
42	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$."
43	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
44	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
45	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
46	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
47	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
48	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
49	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
50	  sheafification of the presheaf
51	  \[

53	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
54	  \]

56	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).

1	\chapter{Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
5	% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
6	% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
7	% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition
8	% \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA))
9	% (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex).
10	
11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G\) for the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\)
33	(a functor \(\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\)), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\)
36	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is
37	left exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).
40	
41	\section{Known results}
42	
43	The following three well-known results underpin the injective-resolution
44	construction of \(R^n G\) and are the foundational inputs to the argument.
45	
46	\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
47	  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
48	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
49	  \mathlibok
50	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
51	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
52	  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
53	  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
54	  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each
55	  \(I^n\) injective), and every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), there is a canonical
56	  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
57	  \[
58	    (R^n G)(X)
59	      \;\cong\;
60	      H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr),
61	  \]
62	  where \(G(I^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex obtained by applying \(G\)
63	  degreewise to the resolution complex \(I^\bullet\) and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th
64	  cohomology object. This is the defining computation of the right-derived functor
65	  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.
66	\end{lemma}
67	
68	\begin{lemma}[Higher derived functors vanish on injectives]
69	  \label{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
70	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ}
71	  \mathlibok
72	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
73	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(J\) is an injective object of
74	  \(\mathcal{A}\) then \((R^{n+1} G)(J) = 0\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\); that
75	  is, the higher right-derived functors vanish on injective objects. In particular
76	  every injective object is right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of
77	  Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}.
78	\end{lemma}
79	
80	\begin{lemma}[The zeroth derived functor of a left-exact functor]
81	  \label{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
82	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf}
83	  \mathlibok
84	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
85	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(G\) is left exact (preserves
86	  finite limits) then the natural transformation \(G \to R^0 G\) is an isomorphism,
87	  \[
88	    R^0 G \;\cong\; G.

179	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
180	
181	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
182	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
183	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
184	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
185	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
186	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
187	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
188	lift.
189	
190	\subsection{The horseshoe construction, step by step}
191	
192	The construction proceeds by induction on degree; we isolate its independent moving
193	parts as the sub-lemmas below, from which the horseshoe lemma is assembled. Throughout,
194	\(I_A\) and \(I_C\) are fixed injective resolutions of \(A\) and \(C\) (they exist
195	because \(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives), and we write
196	\(d_A, d_C\) for their differentials and \(I_A^n, I_C^n\) for their degree-\(n\) terms.
197	The middle resolution is built degreewise as the biproduct
198	\(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\). All seven statements are structural pieces of the dual
199	Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to Homological Algebra}, Lemma
200	2.2.8); no new source is introduced.
201	
202	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: a finite biproduct of injectives is injective]
203	  \label{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
204	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Injective.instBiprod}
205	  \mathlibok
206	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
207	  In an abelian category, if \(P\) and \(Q\) are injective objects then their biproduct
208	  \(P \oplus Q\) is injective. Consequently each degree term
209	  \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) of the middle complex is injective, being the
210	  biproduct of the injective terms \(I_A^n\) and \(I_C^n\) of the two chosen
211	  resolutions.
212	\end{lemma}
213	
214	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: the biproduct short complex is split exact]
215	  \label{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
216	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.Splitting.ofHasBinaryBiproduct}
217	  \mathlibok
218	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
219	  For objects \(P, Q\) of an abelian category the short complex
220	  \[
221	    P \xrightarrow{\ \iota_P\ } P \oplus Q \xrightarrow{\ \pi_Q\ } Q
222	  \]
223	  formed from the biproduct coprojection \(\iota_P\) and projection \(\pi_Q\) carries a
224	  canonical \texttt{ShortComplex.Splitting}; in particular it is a split short exact
225	  sequence. Applied in each degree \(n\) with \(P = I_A^n\), \(Q = I_C^n\), this exhibits
226	  every degree
227	  \[
228	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n \longrightarrow 0
229	  \]
230	  as split, furnishing the degreewise-splitting data required for the construction.
231	\end{lemma}

233	\begin{lemma}\leanok
234	[Per-stage monomorphism into the biproduct]
235	  \label{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
236	  \lean{CategoryTheory.mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact}
237	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
238	  Let \(0 \to S.X_1 \xrightarrow{S.f} S.X_2 \xrightarrow{S.g} S.X_3 \to 0\) be an exact
239	  short complex with \(S.f\) a monomorphism, and let \(\alpha : S.X_1 \to P\),
240	  \(\gamma : S.X_3 \to Q\) be monomorphisms with \(P\) injective. Then the map
241	  \[
242	    \bigl\langle\, \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)\,,\; S.g \circ \gamma \,\bigr\rangle
243	      \;:\; S.X_2 \longrightarrow P \oplus Q,
244	  \]
245	  whose first component is the injective extension of \(\alpha\) along the mono \(S.f\)
246	  and whose second component is \(S.X_2 \twoheadrightarrow S.X_3 \hookrightarrow Q\), is a
247	  monomorphism. This is the kernel/cokernel step driving each stage of the recursion:
248	  applied to the \(n\)-th cosyzygy short exact sequence with \(P = I_A^{n+1}\),
249	  \(Q = I_C^{n+1}\) it produces the monomorphism of the next cosyzygy into the next
250	  biproduct term, whose cokernel feeds the following stage (and, at the base stage with
251	  the original sequence \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\), the augmentation
252	  \(\beta : B \hookrightarrow I_B^0\)).
253	\end{lemma}
254	
255	\begin{proof}
256	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
257	  \leanok
258	  Write \(u := \langle \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f), S.g \circ \gamma\rangle\). To see
259	  \(u\) is mono it suffices, in an abelian category, to show \(x \circ u = 0 \implies x = 0\)
260	  for any \(x\). Composing \(x \circ u\) with the two biproduct projections gives
261	  \(x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\) and \(x \circ S.g \circ \gamma = 0\);
262	  since \(\gamma\) is mono the latter yields \(x \circ S.g = 0\). Exactness of \(S\) at
263	  \(S.X_2\) (with \(S.f\) mono) lets us lift \(x\) through \(S.f\): there is \(y\) with
264	  \(y \circ S.f = x\). Then \(y \circ \alpha = y \circ S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)
265	  = x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\), using the defining property
266	  \(S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = \alpha\); as \(\alpha\) is mono, \(y = 0\),
267	  hence \(x = y \circ S.f = 0\).
268	\end{proof}

312	  previous-stage identity \(\tau^{n} \circ d_C^{n-1} = -\,d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1}\) gives

320	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) by construction. This is exactly
321	  the dual of the inductive lift in the Horseshoe Lemma.

448	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^\bullet \longrightarrow I_B^\bullet
449	      \longrightarrow I_C^\bullet \longrightarrow 0 .
450	  \]
451	  Apply the complex-level homology long exact sequence
452	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}). The outer complexes \(I_A^\bullet\),
453	  \(I_C^\bullet\) are injective resolutions, hence exact in every positive degree
454	  (\(H^{k}(I_A) = H^{k}(I_C) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\)) with \(H^0(I_A) \cong A\),
455	  \(H^0(I_C) \cong C\). In the long exact sequence the terms flanking \(H^{k}(I_B)\) for
456	  \(k \geq 1\) vanish, forcing \(H^{k}(I_B) = 0\); in degree \(0\) the initial segment
457	  \(0 \to H^0(I_A) \to H^0(I_B) \to H^0(I_C) \to H^1(I_A) = 0\) reads
458	  \(0 \to A \to H^0(I_B) \to C \to 0\), which together with the original sequence
459	  \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\) and the augmentation \(\beta\) identifies
460	  \(H^0(I_B) \cong B\). Hence \(I_B^\bullet\) is exact in positive degrees and resolves
461	  \(B\); with injective terms it is an injective resolution of \(B\).
462	\end{proof}

481	  \]

484	  \[

499	  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8), assembled from the sub-lemmas of \S``The horseshoe

501	  injective by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}, degreewise split by
502	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}. The twist \(\tau\) and augmentation exist with
503	  the cocycle identity (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, via
504	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}); the matrix differential then squares to zero

512	\section{The dimension-shift step}
513	
514	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
515	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
516	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
517	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
518	complex-level homology long exact sequence
519	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
520	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We first record the shift at the
521	\emph{resolution} level — where the horseshoe lift directly supplies the
522	degreewise-split short exact sequence of injective resolutions — and then specialize
523	to objects.
524	
525	\begin{lemma}\leanok
526	[Resolution-level dimension shift]
527	  \label{lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
528	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES}
529	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
530	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
531	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
532	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\) be a short exact sequence with \(J\) right-\(G\)-acyclic,
533	  lifted to a \emph{degreewise-split} short exact sequence of injective resolutions
534	  \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\) (chain maps \(\varphi, \psi\) with
535	  \(\psi \circ \varphi = 0\) and a splitting of \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\)
536	  in each degree \(n\)). Then for every \(k \geq 0\) the connecting map of the homology
537	  long exact sequence of \(G(I_\bullet)\) is an isomorphism
538	  \[
539	    (R^{k+1} G)(Z) \;\cong\; (R^{k+2} G)(A).
540	  \]
541	\end{lemma}
542	
543	\begin{proof}
544	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
545	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
546	    \leanok
547	  Applying \(G\) degreewise to the degreewise-split sequence preserves each split
548	  degree, yielding a short exact sequence of cochain complexes
549	  \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). Its homology long
550	  exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) has connecting maps
551	  \(\delta^m : H^m(G(I_Z)) \to H^{m+1}(G(I_A))\); transport these across the
552	  injective-resolution computation \(H^m(G(I_-)) \cong (R^m G)(-)\)
553	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). For \(m = k+1 \geq 1\) the two
554	  homology objects flanking \(\delta^m\) in the long exact sequence both sit on the
555	  middle complex \(G(I_J)\), namely \(H^{k+1}(G(I_J))\) and \(H^{k+2}(G(I_J))\); both
556	  vanish because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}).
557	  With both neighbours zero, \(\delta^{k+1}\) is an isomorphism, giving
558	  \((R^{k+1} G)(Z) \cong (R^{k+2} G)(A)\).
559	\end{proof}
